coredata.dc:title: Density functional theory study on optical and electronic properties of co-doped graphene quantum dots based on different nitrogen doping patterns
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Jianguang
Guo, Qian
Song, Na
Liu, Haiying
Dong, Hongzhou
Chen, Yingjie
Yu, Liyan
Dong, Lifeng
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom doping, especially co-doping, is an effective way to tailor electronic structures of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with synergistic effects and desirable properties. However, due to different synthesis methods, the widespread use of GQDs co-doped with heteroatoms is hindered by the poor understanding of their optical properties and mechanisms. In this work, co-doped GQDs based on three N-doping configurations are chosen to reveal underlying mechanisms of optical properties using density functional theory and time-dependent density functional theory calculations. Based on different N-doping patterns, B, P and S atoms can endow GQDs with a wide spectrum of new optical properties and electronic structures. The HOMO–LUMO gaps of N-doped GQDs with graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N are 0.77, 0.25 and 2.69 eV, respectively. In the co-doped GQDs, B, P and S containing functional groups cause low absorptions in the range of 400 to 800 nm and multiple absorption peaks at about 400 and 600 nm, while the N atom affects the position and intensity of prominent absorption peak according to three different N-doping patterns. The B atom forms sp2 hybridization in the graphene lattice, while the P and S atoms transform the sp2 hybridized carbon into the sp3 state. It is anticipated that this work will provide valuable insights for understanding absorption mechanisms and electronic properties of heteroatom co-doped GQDs as well as achieving new applications with well-defined and desired properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Heteroatom
Co-doping
Optical property
Electronic structure
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Energetic stability and chemical reactivity descriptors

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The absorption spectra of the GQDs with diverse heteroatom doping patterns.
The calculated HOMO–LUMO energy gap of the GQDs with different heteroatom doped patterns.
Molecular orbital surfaces of HOMO and LUMO in doped GQDs based on pristine GQD and GQD-Nh in the ground state. The red and green color represents positive and negative orbital lobes, respectively.

The NTO pairs for the dominant excited state of doped GQDs based on pristine GQD and GQD-Nh. For this state the “hole” is on the bottom, the “particle” is on the top; the λi mean the associated eigenvalues of corresponding NTOs.

Representation of the electron difference density between the dominated excited state minus ground state for doped GQDs based on pristine GQD and GQD–Nh. At the bottom, the lateral views are shown. (The isovalue is 4*10−4.)
The chemical formula, cohesive energy E c, chemical hardness η, chemical potential μ and electrophilicity index ω of doped GQDs with different heteroatom doped patterns.
The transition coefficients and associated eigenvalues of the dominated excitation in doped GQDs based on pristine GQD and GQD–Nh.

head.ce:title: Density functional theory study on optical and electronic properties of co-doped graphene quantum dots based on different nitrogen doping patterns

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom doping, especially co-doping, is an effective way to tailor electronic structures of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with synergistic effects and desirable properties. However, due to different synthesis methods, the widespread use of GQDs co-doped with heteroatoms is hindered by the poor understanding of their optical properties and mechanisms. In this work, co-doped GQDs based on three N-doping configurations are chosen to reveal underlying mechanisms of optical properties using density functional theory and time-dependent density functional theory calculations. Based on different N-doping patterns, B, P and S atoms can endow GQDs with a wide spectrum of new optical properties and electronic structures. The HOMO–LUMO gaps of N-doped GQDs with graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N are 0.77, 0.25 and 2.69 eV, respectively. In the co-doped GQDs, B, P and S containing functional groups cause low absorptions in the range of 400 to 800 nm and multiple absorption peaks at about 400 and 600 nm, while the N atom affects the position and intensity of prominent absorption peak according to three different N-doping patterns. The B atom forms sp2 hybridization in the graphene lattice, while the P and S atoms transform the sp2 hybridized carbon into the sp3 state. It is anticipated that this work will provide valuable insights for understanding absorption mechanisms and electronic properties of heteroatom co-doped GQDs as well as achieving new applications with well-defined and desired properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional materials with distinct electronic and optical properties for potential applications in bioimaging, photovoltaic and light emitters [1–3]. Optical properties, such as ultraviolet (UV) absorption and photoluminescence (PL), are among various attractive properties of GQDs with quantum confinement and edge effects [4]. While actual optical mechanisms of GQDs are still debated among researchers, a general perspective exists that the size and surface chemistry of GQDs have a significant role in determining their optical and electronic properties. Thus, controlling these factors precisely is the key to get GQDs with well-defined and desirable properties. Recently, some works have devoted to the synthesis of selective and size sensitive GQD structures. It is demonstrated that optical properties of GQDs can be tailored through controlling synthesis strategies [5,6], precursors [7] and reaction temperatures [8] to effectively produce GQDs with various sizes. Also, the optical properties of GQDs can be modulated through surface modulation and chemical doping. In addition, heteroatom doping could be an effective strategy to engineer optical and electron transfer properties due to structural and electronic distortions. Doping foreign atoms can affect the overall electrical distribution of GQDs to tune electronic and optical properties. To date, various heteroatoms, including nitrogen (N) [9], sulphur (S) [10], boron (B) [11], phosphorus (P) [12] and halogen (Cl) [13] elements, have been researched to improve properties of GQDs for further applications. N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) are the most widely studied as the N atom has a comparable atomic size and an extra valence electron for bonding with carbon atoms. Up to date, the reports on the N-GQDs have demonstrated that the introduction of N atom can successfully tune intrinsic properties for advanced device application [14–16]. The B atom has similar atomic radium though fewer valence electrons with respect to N, so B-doping would also alter optical properties of GQDs and induce novel and complementary properties. B-doped GQDs (B-GQDs) could simply be synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal process and exhibited uniform size and fluorescence. It demonstrated that B-GQDs can serve as a fluorescence probe towards Fe3+ ion [17]. Also, the B-GQDs showed a better power conversion efficiency for dye-sensitized solar cells when B-GQDs were incorporated to ZnO [18]. To address the mechanism underlying emission characteristics of N-GQDs and B-GQDs, the effects of N- and B-doping on the PL emission were elucidated based on spectroscopic methods and theoretical calculations. The N-GQDs and B-GQDs exhibited blue shift and red shift respectively due to the alteration of the whole electronic structure [19]. The electronic and optical properties of S/N-doped GQDs were investigated based on density functional theory (DFT), which indicated that edge doping was more favorable than center doping for foreign atom doping. This statement was in good agreement with experimental observations that heteroatom-doped GQDs could lead to red or blue shift in UV–Vis absorption [20].

Recently, co-doped GQDs with dual heteroatoms emerged as a hot research topic. S, N-codoped GQDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted thermal decomposition. Compared with N-doped GQDs, the S, N-codoped GQDs showed prominent absorption in the region of 550–595 nm to improve the use of solar light for energy conversion [21]. A strategy was proposed for the preparation of N, P-codoped GQDs with a bright blue-green emission to enhance photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanoparticles for dye degradations [22]. Compared with single heteroatom doped GQDs, GQDs doped with dual heteroatoms can cover a wide range of chemical and physical properties owing to large heterogeneity. However, an urgent need is to comprehend electronic and optical properties of co-doped GQDs with dual heteroatoms. Theoretical calculation is a valid method to isolate different factors and construct specific structures for a better and more comprehensive understanding of GQDs with novel properties [23–26]. It has been demonstrated that properties of graphene and GQDs are highly dependent on synthesis approaches, such as preparation procedures, reactants, temperature, etc. It is worth mentioning that graphene structure can be endowed with different bond configurations through temperature control. For example, at higher pyrolysis temperatures, more stable doping configurations are formed in graphene structure. For the N-doped graphene, the N-doping configuration can be tuned by adjusting the doping temperatures. For instance, the transformation of pyrrolic N and pyridinic N into graphitic N occurred at 600 to 800 °C [27]. Wang et al. reported that with the increase of temperature, N content decreased while S content increased, indicating that effective S doping requires higher temperatures in N, S-codoped graphene structure [28]. For N and P dopants, both P and N doping levels decreased as a function of temperature. The amount of PC bonds increased but that of PO bonds decreased with an increase in the temperature from 700 to 1000 °C [29]. As mentioned above, the formation of heteroatom doping in GQDs can be tailored by controlling temperature during synthesis progress. In addition, optoelectronic properties of GQDs are closely associated with the bonding configurations in their heteroatom doping. Hence, a good understanding on the correlation between optoelectronic properties and bonding configurations of GQDs is critical for researchers to develop methods for controllably synthesizing GQDs with desirable doping configurations and properties for different applications. Hence, in this work, we employed DFT and time dependent (TD)-DFT to investigate optical and electronic properties of co-doped GQDs with the aim to explore optical mechanisms of N/B, N/P and N/S co-doped GQDs based on three N-doping configurations: graphitic N, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N. The results will guide the synthesis of more efficient GQDs for potential applications in solar cells and optoelectronic devices.

It is known that the cohesive energy is the energy used to break a molecule into isolated atoms by destroying all bonds [44]. Hence, cohesive energies per atom E c of N-GQDs and co-doped GQDs are calculated to evaluate the stability of these structures as follows:
(1)
E C
=
E
N − GQD
−
N C
E C
−
N N
E N
−
N H
E H
N C
+
N N
+
N H
(2)
E C
=
E
N / X
co − doped
GQD
−
N C
E C
−
N N
E N
−
N O
E O
−
N X
E X
−
N H
E H
N C
+
N N
+
N O
+
N X
+
N H
where E N-GQD and E N/X co-doped GQD denote the total energy of the N-doped and N/X-codoped GQD (X = B, P and S), E C, E N, E O, E X and E H are the atomic energy of C, N, O, X and H, respectively. N C, N N, N O, N X and N H are the number of C, N, O, X and H.

The possibility of designing and manipulating properties of GQDs by controlling their shape and components has made GQDs one of the most versatile materials. The chemical reactivity parameters [45–48], such as chemical hardness η [49], chemical potential μ [50] and electrophilicity index ω [51,52], are very useful to study reactivity trend, excited states and analyzing toxicity [53,54]. The quantitative definitions for chemical potential and chemical hardness can respectively be given as:
(3)
η = IP − EA
(4)
μ = −
IP + EA
2
(5)
ω =
μ 2
2 η

The ionization potential IP and electron affinity EA can be approximated using Koopmans' theorem [55]:
(6)
IP = −
E HOMO
(7)
EA = −
E LUMO
where E HOMO and E LUMO are the energy levels of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energetic stability and chemical reactivity descriptors
ce:section.ce:para: The cohesive energy per atom is closely related to electronic structure, which is the difference of the total molecular energy and the sum of constituent atomic energies. Larger negative values endorse more stability of investigated molecules. In our work, the cohesive energy per atom of pristine GQDs and doped GQDs are tabulated in Table 1. The negative value of cohesive energy per atom indicates the stability of doped GQDs. As shown in Table 1, compared with pristine GQD, the E c of GQD–Ng is higher, while those of GQD–Np and GQD–Nh are lower. In addition, GQD–Nh has the highest negative value of cohesive energy, which indicates the most stable structure with high chemical stability. The decreasing trend of cohesive energy of the N doped GQDs is GQD–Ng > GQD–Np > GQD–Nh. The cohesive energy value of GQDs only doped with B, P or S decreases as GQD–PC2O > GQD–SC2O > GQD–BC2O. For co-doped GQDs, the trend is similar when GQDs are doped with B, P or S on the same kind of N pattern. For example, the cohesive energy value decreases in the order GQD–NhPC2O > GQD–NhSC2O > GQD–NhBC2O.

The chemical hardness of a system is one of highly useful concepts to understand its reactivity. A larger value leads to lower polarizability, less reactive, and more stable. The chemical hardness has the same value as the HOMO–LUMO gap. Hard molecules thus have large HOMO–LUMO gap and soft molecules have small HOMO–LUMO gap [46]. As shown in Table 1, the chemical hardness value of GQDs is distinctly reduced through heteroatom doping. In addition, the hardness of GQD–NhPC2O, GQD–NhSC2O and GQD–NhBC2O is decreased compared with that of GQD–Nh. While the value of co-doped GQDs on graphitic and pyrrolic N patterns is enhanced than that of GQD–Ng and GQD–Np, respectively. It is worth noting that the hardness change trend of co-doped GQDs is difference based on different N doping patterns. The chemical potential is the negative of electronegativity and provides a measure of the intrinsic strength of attracting electrons. A good electron acceptor has low chemical potential. As can be seen in Table 1, the change range of chemical potentials is relatively small among the doped GQDs. The N/S co-doped GQDs have the lowest value among the co-doped GQDs with B, P or S based on the same N doping pattern, which may be attributed to that the S atom has extra electrons to attract other electrons. The electrophilicity index of a system measures its propensity to absorb electrons and characterizes its electron gaining power. Hence, electrophilicity can be regarded as the intrinsic property of a material of being an electrophile. A molecule with higher global electrophilicity value would be more reactive. Electrophilicity index of co-doped GQDs is remarkably changed when B, P or S is introduced onto N-doped GQDs. The electrophilicity is increased when B, P or S is added to pyridinic N doped GQDs while reduced values are found for graphitic and pyrrolic N doped GQDs. This means that heteroatom doping can be considered as an efficient approach to tune the electrophilicity of GQDs. As discussed, these reactivity descriptors could give a better appreciation of underlying properties associated with electronic structure principles of GQDs.

Fig. 1 shows the UV–vis spectra of GQDs with diverse heteroatom-doping patterns including both single and co-doping. The UV–vis spectra of all doped GQDs exhibit a large shift and weakened strength. Fig. 1a shows the UV–vis spectra of pristine GQD and the GQDs doped with BC2O, PC2O and SC2O. The pristine GQD has a higher absorption around 450 nm than all doped GQDs, but no significant absorption in the region of 600 to 800 nm. The GQDs doped with BC2O, PC2O and SC2O have low absorption in the visible light region (400–800 nm) and with multiple absorption peaks. Comparing with the GQDs only doped with B, P or S (Fig. 1a), the spectral profile is changed differently when co-doping with different N-doping configurations (Fig. 1b–d). For example, the absorption spectra of GQD–BC2O, GQD–PC2O and GQD–SC2O show low intensity in visible region and a stretched tail down to near infrared region. However, the absorption intensity of GQD–NgBC2O, GQD–NgPC2O and GQD–NgSC2O is enhanced evidently at about 450 nm, and the spectral shape and main peaks are similar to those of GQD–Ng, which indicates the graphitic N plays an important role on tuning optical properties of GQD–NgBC2O, GQD–NgPC2O and GQD–NgSC2O (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, compared with GQD–Ng, the absorption intensity of GQD–NgBC2O is enhanced while the intensities of GQD–NgPC2O and GQD–NgSC2O are reduced. For the pyrrolic N-doping, the absorption main peak of GQD–NpBC2O has an obvious blue-shifted phenomenon compared with GQD–Np, while those of GQD–NpPC2O and GQD–NpSC2O exhibit red shift (Fig. 1c). As shown in Fig. 1d, optical absorption properties of GQD–Nh are similar to those of pristine GQDs, while GQD–NhBC2O, GQD–NhPC2O and GQD–NhSC2O exhibit different absorption spectra. Compared with GQD–Nh, the absorption spectrum of GQD–NhBC2O has an evident red shift, and those of GQD–NhPC2O and GQD–NhSC2O exhibit blue shift with a shoulder peak. It is worth noting that the co-doping of B, P or S shows opposite effects on the spectra of GQD–Np and GQD–Nh. The results above suggest that the co-doping of B, P or S can affect the position and intensity of prominent absorption peaks of N-doped GQDs differently based on different N doping configurations. Additionally, B-doping performs differently from P or S doping, which can be ascribed to the fact that P and S have more valence electrons than B.

As we know, ZINDO/S [56] approach is a semi-empirical electronic structure method, which has been proved to successfully predict optical properties of polyaromatic hydrocarbons [44,57]. Hence, we used the ZINDO/S approach as implemented in Gaussian 09 to calculate the maximum absorption wavelength and corresponding transition energy for N-doped GQDs and N/S co-doped GQDs. As given in Table S3, for pristine GQD and N-doped GQDs, the maximum absorption wavelength and transition energy value calculated by ZINDO/S and TD-DFT are very close. However, as for N/S co-doped GQD, the results are quite different. This result is consistent with previous reports that ZINDO/S is mainly employed to predict optical properties of the systems only containing C, N, O, H atoms like polyaromatic hydrocarbons [57,58].

The energy levels, such as HOMO and LUMO, correlate with the absorption spectra. Hence, the two contiguous energy levels of pristine and doped GQDs are depicted to reveal effects of different co-doping patterns on GQDs (Fig. 2 ). Compared to pristine GQD, the HOMO–LUMO gap of single and co-doped GQDs is greatly reduced. It demonstrates that the co-doped patterns with different heteroatoms can tune the HOMO and LUMO levels of the GQDs directly. As shown in Fig. 2a, the HOMO–LUMO gap has obvious reduction through B, P or S doping, which is consistent with the variation of absorption spectra in Fig. 1a. It further demonstrates that the B, P or S doping can play a dominating effect on tuning optical properties of GQDs. For the N-doped GQDs, three N-doping configurations have distinctive different impact on affecting the HOMO–LUMO gaps (Fig. 2b–d). The HOMO–LUMO gaps of N-doped GQDs with Ng, Np and Nh are 0.77, 0.25 and 2.69 eV, respectively. Compared with pristine GQD, the HOMO–LUMO gap of GQD-Nh is nearly unchanged, while the HOMO–LUMO gaps of GQD–Ng and GQD–Np become much smaller. This result indicates that the change of HOMO–LUMO gaps corresponds to the alteration of UV–vis absorption spectra. Both absorption spectrum and energy gap of GQD–Nh are similar to those of pristine GQD. Also, the absorption spectra of GQD–Ng and GQD–Np show different alterations when their HOMO–LUMO gaps exhibit drastic variations. The HOMO and LUMO levels of GQD–Np are both compressed, while only the HOMO level of GQD–Ng is raised obviously. For co-doped GQDs, the HOMO–LUMO gaps of N/B, N/P and N/S co-doped GQDs have a similar alteration trend while the alteration depends on the N-doping patterns. For example, the HOMO–LUMO gaps of N/B co-doped GQDs with Ng, Np and Nh are 1.81, 0.59 and 0.42 eV, and the gaps of N-doped GQDs with Ng, Np and Nh are 0.77, 0.25 and 2.69 eV, respectively. Compared with N-doped GQDs, the HOMO–LUMO gaps of GQD–NgBC2O and GQD–NpBC2O are enlarged due to the reduced HOMO level, but the gap of GQD–NhBC2O exhibits obvious compressed based on GQD–Nh. Furthermore, compared with GQD–BC2O, the HOMO–LUMO gap of GQD–NhBC2O is almost unchanged, and the scope of gap change in N/B co-doped GQD is GQD–NgBC2O > GQD–NpBC2O > GQD–NhBC2O, which indicates that the contribution of BC2O is pronounced in regulating the HOMO–LUMO gap of co-doped GQDs with pyridinic N doping. For the GQD–NgBC2O, the graphitic N doping also holds a vital role in tuning the HOMO–LUMO gap besides B-doping.

It is important to emphasize that orbital is very useful for qualitative descriptions of frontier orbital hybridization. Fig. 3 represents isosurfaces of the HOMO and LUMO to evaluate the electronic structure of GQDs. In this section, we take the co-doped patterns based on pristine GQD and GQD–Nh as example, the other two co-doped patterns are displayed in Fig. S1 of SI. From the analysis of spatial distribution of isosurfaces, the molecular orbital of pristine GQD (Fig. 3a) display clear π properties, and the spatial distribution of GQD–Nh (Fig. 3e) remains delocalization. These distributions suggest that pyridinic N-doping has limited impact on electronic structures. For the GQD–Np and GQD–Ng in Fig. S1 of SI, the spatial distribution of electrons exhibits a distinct localization, which is manifest that pyrrolic and graphitic N-doping induces a creation of hybridization in GQD plane. Especially, for the HOMO and LUMO of the pyrrolic N-doped GQDs (Fig. S1a–d of SI), the spatial distributions of LUMO are almost unchanged even through co-doping with B, P, or S. Hence, the effect of pyrrolic N on tuning the electronic structure of co-doped GQDs is more important than that of B, P, or S-doping. Noteworthy, the HOMO and LUMO of GQD–BC2O, GQD–PC2O and GQD–SC2O exhibit similar spatial distributions (Fig. 3b–d). This phenomenon also can be observed in co-doped GQDs with three different N-doping patterns. However, electronic structures around three different heteroatoms show minute difference in co-doped GQDs. For example, B/P/S co-doping plays an important role on tuning optical property of pyridinic N-doped GQD (Fig. 3f–h), the electron spatial distribution surrounding B atom has a clear π property (Fig. 3f), but the distribution around P or S atom has the property of σ orbit (Fig. 3g and h). It indicates that the B atom forms sp2 hybridization in the carbon lattices and the planar structure of graphene retains. While the P and S atoms transform the sp2 hybridized carbon into the sp3 state for radii of P and S atoms are larger than that of B. This further demonstrates that B atom has different effects on tuning optical and structural properties of co-doped GQD compared with P and S atoms.

In order to illustrate co-doping effects on GQDs, excitation energies and oscillator strengths corresponding to the excited state with 25 lowest excited states of GQDs were calculated during absorption process. The data are listed in Tables S4–S19 of SI. Absorption spectra are associated with orbital transition at vertical excitation. In order to evaluate electronic transitions in absorption process, the dominated excited states are selected with major absorption bands in optical spectrum. Table 2 represents the orbital transition coefficients of dominated excitation for the doped GQDs based on pristine GQD and GQD–Nh, and the other doped GQDs based on GQD–Np and GQD–Ng are listed in Table S20 of SI. For pristine GQD, the dominant excited state is composed of H − 1 → L + 1 (48.02%) and H → L (48.02%), where H and L indicate the molecular orbitals and bracketed data indicates the contribution of orbital transition. As we know, the HOMO and HOMO−1 as well as the LUMO and LUMO+1 are twofold degenerate in pristine GQD, which means the orbital transitions that participate in excitation is dominated by a π→π* transition. However, the excitations for doped GQDs exhibit various patterns of orbital transitions and the contributions of excitation are likewise multiply as shown in Table 2. For example, the dominant excitation of GQD–PC2O is a mixed transition among four prominent orbital transition pairs: H − 3 → L(13.5%), H − 2 → L(44.2%), H − 1 → L + 1(7.2%) and H → L + 1(20.5%). However, it is not definitive to analyze the process of transitions corresponding to mixed orbital transitions along with their respective coefficients. Another helpful technique is to examine the natural transition orbitals (NTOs) [59]. One or two associated eigenvalues of the NTO can be computed rapidly using transition densities produced by the TD-DFT energy calculations (Tables 2 and S20 in SI). As shown in Table 2, λi is the associated eigenvalue reflecting the importance of a particular hole-particle excitation to overall transitions for the dominant excitation. Fig. 4 illustrates the NTOs corresponding to dominant singlet transitions of the doped GQDs based on pristine GQD and GQD–Nh with the associated eigenvalues λi, and the other doped GQDs based on GQD–Np and GQD–Ng are given in Fig. S2 of SI. The electron cloud in pristine GQDs with S0 → S3 transition needs two pairs of NTO to describe the hole and particle disruption (Fig. 4a). The hole and particle disruptions exhibit delocalization and have the same associated eigenvalue with λi = 0.48, which corresponds to the dominant transition of pristine GQD as a π→π* transition. For the doped GQDs, the transition character is various due to different doping patterns. For example, only one hole–particle pair can describe the electron cloud of transition in GQD–BC2O with S0 → S18, but the transitions of GQD–PC2O for S0 → S23 and S0 → S5 need two pairs. It is proved that the transition mechanism of doped GQDs with different heteroatom-doping is different even they have similar variation on absorption spectra and energy levels. For GQD–Nh, the hole and particle disruptions exhibit delocalization and have a near eigenvalue with λi = 0.52 and 0.43, which indicates pyridinic N atom has little impact on tuning electronic structure of GQD in transition process. Compared with B/P/S-doped GQDs, the NTO pairs of GQD–NhBC2O, GQD–NhPC2O and GQD–NhSC2O have no change to describe the excited state transition, which demonstrates the N/P/S-doping plays a primary role in transition process based on GQD–Nh and pristine GQD. For the co-doped GQDs based on GQD–Ng (Fig. S2f–h of SI), the dominate transition of GQD–NgBC2O, GQD–NgPC2O and GQD–NgSC2O only needs one hole–particle pair to describe electron cloud, which can be attributed to the combined effect of graphitic N and B/P/S co-doping.

In order to further analyze the mechanism of optical properties corresponding to excited states, the charge difference density (CDD) is used to describe charge transfer in the dominate transition. The CDDs of doped GQDs based on pristine GQD and GQD–Nh are comparatively shown in Fig. 5 , and the populations of the other doped GQDs is exhibited in Fig. S3 of SI. The CDD represents density difference between the excited state minus the ground state. The blue region indicates the electron density in the ground state is lower than that in the excited state. The CDD of pristine GQD reveals that the electron–hole pairs are delocalized and the adjacent holes through the whole molecule to transfer electrons. Thus, this electronic transition is locally excited. For the GQD–Nh, the influence in charge population induced by N-doping decreases the delocalization of hole and electron distribution, but the electronic transition remains locally excited. Compared with the electronic transition density of GQD–NhBC2O, GQD–NhPC2O and GQD–NhSC2O (Fig. 5f–h), the hole–electron distribution of GQD–NhBC2O is locally excited, while those of GQD–NhPC2O and GQD–NhSC2O are divided into two separated regions, which are similar to those of GQD–BC2O, GQD–PC2O and GQD–SC2O, respectively (Fig. 5b–d). These figures demonstrate that B/P/S-doping has a more important effect than N-doping in the electronic transition of co-doped GQDs based on pyridinic N atom. Compared with GQD–NhBC2O, P and S atoms form pyramidal-like bonding configurations with other atoms to induce more structural and electronic distortions.

In this work, we analyzed the variations of heteroatom co-doping on optical absorption process of GQDs based on electronic structures. Heteroatoms (i.e., B, P, S) and three N-doping patterns were investigated through TD-DFT. The trend of absorption spectral change for co-doped GQDs can be opposite for certain N-doping patterns. For example, compared with GQD–Np, the main absorption peak of GQD–NpBC2O has an evident blue shift while those of GQD–NpPC2O and GQD–NpSC2O exhibit red shift. However, compared with GQD–Nh, the absorption spectrum of GQD–NhBC2O exhibits red shift while those of GQD–NhPC2O and GQD–NhSC2O turn to blue shift. Through the electron transition analysis, it was found that the effect of Np on tuning the electronic structure of co-doped GQDs is more important than those of B/P/S-doping. While the B/P/S-doping is pronounced in tuning the HOMO–LUMO gap of co-doped GQDs with Nh doping pattern. It is expected that this work will provide valuable knowledge for understanding and interpreting electronic and optical properties of GQDs co-doped with different atoms as well as give important insights and guidance for the development of methods to controllably synthesize GQDs with well-defined and desirable properties towards specific purposes. It is anticipated that this work will provide valuable information for the understanding and interpretation of optical and electronic properties of GQDs co-doped with different atoms, and get the insight of these phenomena and their originations to improve the performance for the applications in various fields.
All data used to generate the results in this paper can be obtained from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

Jianguang Feng: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing-original draft. Qian Guo: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation, Writing-review & editing. Na Song: Investigation, Writing-review & editing. Haiying Liu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Hongzhou Dong: Investigation, Writing-original draft, Validation, Formal analysis. Yingjie Chen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing-review & editing. Liyan Yu: Investigation, Writing-review & editing, Validation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Lifeng Dong: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Investigation, Writing-original draft, Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52002198, 21776147, 21905153 and 61604086), the Qingdao Municipal Science and Technology Bureau (19-6-1-91-nsh), a Project of Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (J17KA013), and the Chemcloudcomputing of National Supercomputing Center in Shenzhen (Shenzhen Cloud Computing Center). L. F. Dong also thanks financial support from the Malmstrom Endowment Fund at Hamline University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108264.
